title: Organisational records: function, activity and creator
audience: Government & corporate archives, institutional records management
intro: Organisational records are best understood through what the body did. This scenario models the records, the creating department, and — the distinctively RiC move — the business Activity that produced them, linking record→activity→agent so provenance is explicit rather than implied by a file path.
question: How do I tie organisational records to the department that made them and the activity that produced them?

steps.prompt: Are you describing a single file, or a series of records from a department?

choices.label: A single file
choices.why: Good — one file is one Record.

choices.label: A series
choices.why: Good — a series of like records is a Record Set, each file a member Record.

choices.label: An Activity
choices.why: Not the records themselves. The business process is an Activity you link the records to — coming up in a later step.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The file is a Record. Its creating department is an Agent; the process that produced it is an Activity it results from.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one file — a Set is for the series.

choices.label: Function
choices.why: A function/activity is what the records document, not the file itself. The file is a Record.

capture.comment: Create the file as a Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. A series of like records (all procurement files, say) is a Record Set, each file a member Record.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — a series is many files. Use a Record Set.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The series is an aggregation, not a component of a record.

capture.comment: Create the series as a Record Set.

choices.label: Corporate body (Agent) + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. A department is a Corporate Body — a kind of Agent. Relate the records to it with has_creator. Model the org hierarchy by relating departments to their parent bodies.

choices.label: A folder name / path
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The creating body should be an Agent so its whole output is findable and its place in the org structure is explicit.

capture.comment: Create the creating department as an Agent (corporate body).
Link the records to the department with has_creator.

steps.prompt: Finally — the business process that produced these records. How do you capture it?

choices.label: Activity, linked with results_from + performed_by
choices.why: Fits — and this is the RiC payoff. Model the process as an Activity; relate the records to it with results_from, and relate the Activity to the department with performed_by. Now provenance is a queryable chain (record → activity → agent), not an implied folder structure.

choices.label: A keyword on the records
choices.why: Too flat. The activity has its own dates, participants and outputs; as a first-class Activity it can link many records and agents.

choices.label: Nothing — the department is enough
choices.why: Misses the function dimension. The same department runs many activities; capturing the activity is what lets you see what the records actually document.

capture.comment: Create the business process as an Activity.
Link the records to the activity with results_from.
Link the activity to the department that performed it.

outcome.verdict: Records, creator and function — a provenance chain.
outcome.summary: The file is a Record (or the series a Record Set); the department is a Corporate-Body Agent; and the business process is an Activity linking record→activity→agent via results_from and performed_by. That chain is RiC's core advantage over a folder tree: provenance is explicit and queryable from any direction.

links.label: See real RiC data in the graph viewer
